title: Bonds
summary: A bond is worth the present value of coupons and par value discounted at the market required return.

topics.title: Bond valuation and interpretation
topics.summary: Learn what each variable means, how each cash flow is discounted, and how to interpret whether a bond trades at a premium, near par, or at a discount.

sections.title: Core idea
sections.body: Bond value comes from discounting each periodic payment and the final principal using the investor's required return. Before using the formula, it helps to read each symbol as a piece of the problem.
bullets: B₀: bond price today. It is the present value you are trying to estimate.
I: annual coupon in money. It represents the periodic cash payment received each year.
N: years to maturity. It tells you how many periods remain until principal is repaid.
k_d: required return. It is the rate demanded by the market based on the bond's risk.
M: par value or principal. It is the amount the issuer repays at the end.

formulaLegend.meaning: bond price today. It is the present value you are trying to calculate.
annual coupon in money. It is the periodic cash payment you receive each year.
years to maturity. It tells you how many periods remain before principal is repaid.
market required return. It is the rate investors demand based on the bond's risk.
par value or principal. It is the amount the issuer repays at the end.

exampleTable.title: Quick reading
exampleTable.footnote: Sensitivity grows as time to maturity becomes longer.

sections.title: What each variable means
sections.body: Before using the formula, it helps to understand what each symbol represents and how it connects to the problem data.

exampleTable.title: Formula variables

cells: This is the present value you want to estimate.
If the bond pays 8% on 1000, then I = 80.
Number of years left until principal is repaid.
Rate the market demands based on the bond's risk.
Amount repaid at maturity, for example 1000.

exampleTable.footnote: This app uses a base annual-coupon model to keep the calculation easier to learn.

sections.title: How to read the formula step by step
sections.body: Bond valuation is easier to understand when you separate the problem into cash flows, time, and discounting.
bullets: First identify the annual coupon I: it is the cash amount you receive each year while the bond is outstanding.
Then locate the horizon N: it tells you how many coupon payments must be discounted before maturity.
Next define k_d: this is the market required return and works as the investor's opportunity cost.
Finally add the present value of all coupons and the present value of principal M.

sections.title: Mini guided case
sections.body: Assume a bond with par value 1000, annual coupon 80, maturity of 5 years, and required return of 10%.

exampleTable.title: Case data and interpretation

cells: The bond pays 80 in interest each year.
You recover principal at the end.
The market demands a return above the implied coupon rate.

exampleTable.footnote: Because the implied coupon rate is 8% and the market requires 10%, price should tend to fall below 1000.

sections.title: Common mistakes when studying bonds
sections.body: Most mistakes come from mixing up rates and amounts or misreading required return.
bullets: Confusing the annual coupon amount with the coupon rate. In this calculator, I is entered as money, not as a percentage.
Using the coupon rate as if it were k_d. Required return comes from the market and the bond's risk, not from the original contract.
Forgetting that principal M is received only once at the end, so it is discounted a single time at N.
Thinking that a bond priced below 1000 is automatically bad. It may simply mean the market requires a higher return.

calculator.title: Bond calculator
calculator.summary: Enter the annual coupon amount, maturity, par value, and required return to estimate the present value of the bond.
calculator.note: Base assumption: this calculator uses annual coupons and repays par value at maturity.

modes.label: Standard valuation
modes.summary: Uses an annual coupon and compound discounting.

fields.label: Annual coupon (amount)
fields.helperText: Annual cash payment. If the bond pays 8% on 1000, enter 80.

fields.label: Par value
fields.helperText: Amount returned by the issuer at maturity.

fields.label: Years to maturity
fields.helperText: Use whole years for this version of the calculator.

fields.label: Required return (%)
fields.helperText: Rate the market demands from the bond based on its risk, not the coupon rate.
